skill.name: routeros-app-yaml
skill.description: RouterOS /app YAML format for container applications (7.21+ builtin app, 7.22+ custom YAML creation). Use when: writing or validating RouterOS /app YAML files, working with MikroTik container apps, building docker-compose-like definitions for RouterOS, creating /app store schemas, debugging /app validation errors, or when the user mentions /app, tikapp, or RouterOS container YAML.
skill.summary: Write and validate YAML configurations for MikroTik RouterOS container applications.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: All 159 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only documentation and YAML configuration examples for RouterOS container applications. There is no executable code, no actual shell commands, no network requests, and no credential handling. The static scanner flagged YAML examples in markdown code blocks as security risks (for example, backticks in YAML values, example URLs in documentation, and placeholder IP addresses in configuration samples). After manual review, no genuine security issues were found.

low_findings.title: False positive: External command detection in YAML documentation
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 132 locations as external command execution. These are YAML configuration examples and markdown documentation, not executable code. Examples include shell commands in YAML entrypoint values (for example, nginx -g daemon off in SKILL.md line 32 and references/examples.md line 31) and backtick-formatted text in markdown. No actual command execution exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both files are pure documentation with YAML code examples in markdown blocks. No executable scripts, no subprocess calls, and no runtime code execution.

low_findings.title: False positive: Network pattern detection in documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 13 locations as network risks including hardcoded URLs, hardcoded IP addresses, and fetch API calls. All are documentation examples: example.com URLs in YAML samples (references/examples.md lines 19, 21), example IP addresses like 192.168.1.1 in port mapping examples (SKILL.md line 110, references/examples.md line 145), and TypeScript fetch snippets showing RouterOS REST API usage (SKILL.md lines 216, 222). No actual network requests are made by this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network patterns appear in markdown code blocks as YAML or TypeScript documentation examples. The skill has no HTTP client code, no network libraries, and no runtime network behavior.

low_findings.title: False positive: Blocker patterns misidentified in YAML content
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged weak cryptographic algorithms, Windows SAM database references, LLM role token injection, and system reconnaissance patterns. None of these patterns exist in the actual file content. The scanner likely misidentified YAML property names or markdown formatting as security threats.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of both files confirms no cryptographic code, no Windows references, no prompt injection, and no system reconnaissance patterns. The files are exclusively RouterOS YAML documentation.

content.value_statement: Create and validate YAML configurations for MikroTik RouterOS container applications. This skill helps you define services, ports, volumes, and networks for the RouterOS /app subsystem without needing docker-compose knowledge.
actual_capabilities: Write valid RouterOS /app YAML with correct service properties, port formats, and volume definitions
Explain differences between RouterOS /app YAML and standard docker-compose syntax
Validate YAML against RouterOS schema including port format patterns and environment variable rules
Generate complete app definitions with services, configs, volumes, networks, and placeholders
Create app store files with multiple /app definitions for bulk deployment
Troubleshoot common errors such as missing container package or unsupported properties
limitations: Does not execute RouterOS commands or connect to live devices
Does not validate YAML against a live RouterOS instance
Schema knowledge is based on RouterOS 7.22 through 7.23 documentation
Cannot test container runtime behavior or network connectivity

use_cases.title: Define a container application for RouterOS
use_cases.description: A network administrator writes YAML to deploy a monitoring stack with Grafana and Prometheus on a MikroTik router using the /app subsystem.

use_cases.title: Migrate docker-compose projects to RouterOS
use_cases.description: A developer adapts an existing docker-compose configuration to RouterOS /app YAML format, adjusting port syntax and removing unsupported properties.

use_cases.title: Create an app store for team deployment
use_cases.description: A system engineer builds a tikappstore YAML file with pre-configured applications for consistent deployment across multiple RouterOS devices.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic /app definition
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a RouterOS /app YAML file that runs an nginx container on port 8080 with a named volume for persistent data.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: You need a simple single-container application on your MikroTik router.

prompt_templates.title: Convert docker-compose to RouterOS YAML
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this docker-compose file to RouterOS /app YAML format. Highlight any properties that are not supported or behave differently.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: You have an existing docker-compose project and want to deploy it on RouterOS.

prompt_templates.title: Create a multi-service application
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a RouterOS /app YAML with a web frontend, a PostgreSQL database, and a Redis cache. Include proper volume mounts, network isolation, and health checks.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: You need a production-ready multi-container application with inter-service networking.

prompt_templates.title: Build an app store file
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RouterOS app store YAML file containing three applications: a web server, a database manager, and a monitoring dashboard. Each should have appropriate categories and metadata.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: You manage multiple RouterOS devices and want a centralized app catalog.

output_examples.input: Create a /app YAML for a Home Assistant container with persistent storage on port 8123
output: A YAML file with name, services, volumes, and networks sections.
The web service uses ghcr.io/home-assistant/home-assistant image with port 8123 mapped.
A named volume home-assistant-config is mounted to /config.
The restart policy is set to always for reliability.

output_examples.input: Show the difference between old and new port formats in RouterOS YAML
output: Old OCI-style uses a slash before the protocol: 8080:80/tcp
New RouterOS 7.23 style uses a colon: 8080:80:web:tcp
Both formats are valid. New deployments from 7.23beta2 should prefer the colon style.
Long-form object syntax with target, published, and protocol fields works in all versions.

best_practices: Always use the .tikapp.yaml file extension for custom RouterOS app definitions to distinguish them from generic YAML files.
Use the *.editor.json schema variant during development for better autocompletion, then validate against *.latest.json before deployment.
Test your YAML on a RouterOS CHR virtual machine before deploying to production hardware to catch schema errors early.
anti_patterns: Assuming full docker-compose compatibility. RouterOS /app supports only a subset of compose properties and handles some differently.
Using the version top-level key. RouterOS ignores this key and it serves no purpose in /app YAML.
Mixing port format styles within a single port string. Each port entry must use either the old OCI style or the new colon style exclusively.

faq.question: What RouterOS version do I need for /app YAML?
faq.answer: The /app path is built-in starting in RouterOS 7.21. Custom app creation via /app/add requires RouterOS 7.22 or later. The container extra package must also be installed.

faq.question: Is RouterOS /app YAML the same as docker-compose?
faq.answer: No. RouterOS /app YAML looks similar to docker-compose but supports fewer properties. Some properties like deploy and resources are not supported. Port mapping syntax also differs between the two.

faq.question: What file extension should I use for /app YAML files?
faq.answer: Use .tikapp.yaml for individual app definitions and .tikappstore.yaml for app store files. These conventions help tools and editors apply the correct schema.

faq.question: How do placeholders work in RouterOS YAML?
faq.answer: Placeholders like [accessIP], [accessPort], [containerIP], and [routerIP] are expanded at deploy time by RouterOS. They appear in port mappings, environment values, and config content.

faq.question: Can I use environment variables with lowercase names?
faq.answer: The strict validation schema requires uppercase environment variable names matching the pattern [A-Z_][A-Z0-9_]*. Use the editor schema variant if you need mixed-case variable names.

faq.question: How do I add a custom app to RouterOS?
faq.answer: Use the CLI command /app/add with the yaml-url parameter pointing to your YAML file URL. Alternatively, use the REST API PUT endpoint at /app with the yaml-url in the request body.
